ResearchGate blocked by authentication or CAPTCHA."}, {"source_url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8382447/", "source_title": "Aging Methods-An Evaluation of Their Influence on Bond Strength", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.58, "overall_relevance": 0.22, "year": 2021, "authors": [], "venue": "European Journal of Dentistry", "doi": "10.1055/s-0040-1721906", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "Thermocycling at 20,000 and 30,000 cycles significantly reduced bond strength to enamel via self-etch approach, while NaOCl storage for 1-5 hours increased bond strength.", "direct_quote": "Thermocycling aging (20,000 and 30,000 cycles) significantly reduced the bond strength to enamel using the SE approach. On the contrary, storage with the NaOCl method proved to increase bond strength under the evaluated conditions. Microshear bond strength tests were conducted, and the collected data (MPa) were subjected to three-way analysis of variance", "fact_category": "measurement", "relevance_score": 0.25, "confidence": 0.89, "perspective": "Methodological", "entities": ["thermocycling", "bond strength", "NaOCl", "20000 cycles", "30000 cycles"]}, {"statement": "Three-way ANOVA on 96 specimens showed substrate (p=0.00) and aging method (p=0.00) significantly affected bond strength, but adhesive approach did not (p=0.84), with adhesive/mixed failures predominant.", "direct_quote": "Three-way ANOVA revealed that the factors substrate (p = 0.00) and aging method (p = 0.00) had a significant effect on the bond strength, but the factor adhesive approach did not (p = 0.84). The bond strength in the enamel group for the SE approach was negatively affected under 20,000 and 30,000 thermal cycles. Adhesive/mixed failures were predominant for all groups", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.21, "confidence": 0.91, "perspective": "Methodological", "entities": ["ANOVA", "p-value", "bond strength", "96 specimens"]}, {"statement": "Aging methods tested included water storage at 37 C for 24 hours to 1 year, thermal cycling of 10,000 to 30,000 cycles, and NaOCl storage for 1 to 5 hours to compare adhesion degradation protocols.", "direct_quote": "A total of 96 noncarious human third molars were prepared and randomly divided according to three factors (n = 6): substrate (enamel and dentin), adhesive approach (SE and ER), and aging method (water storage for 24 hours, 6 months, or 1 year; subjected to 10,000, 20,000, or 30,000 thermal cycles; and sodium hypochlorite storage for 1 or 5 hours)", "fact_category": "methodology", "relevance_score": 0.18, "confidence": 0.92, "perspective": "Methodological", "entities": ["water storage", "thermal cycles", "NaOCl", "aging methods"]}], "evidence_summary": "Dental adhesion study comparing aging methods. Marginally relevant: aging method choice significantly affects measured bond strength degradation."}]}
